claim.study_type: Observational (cross-sectional survey with structural equation modeling)

data.source: Self-report questionnaires administered to Israel Defense Forces soldiers
data.wave_or_subset: Four companies (two armored corps, two infantry)
data.notes: Approximately 10–20% of soldiers (new immigrants) were excluded due to language difficulties; response rates were 65% (armored) and 55% (infantry).

method.description: Participants nearing the end of basic training completed questionnaires measuring adult attachment style, trait anxiety, openness to experience, and retrospective leadership-shaping experiences. Item parcels were created and a measurement model tested, followed by structural equation modeling (SEM) using AMOS 5 to assess the hypothesised causal paths between secure attachment, trait anxiety, openness to experience, and leadership experiences. Several alternative models were compared.
steps: Recruit 286 male IDF soldiers from four companies and obtain consent.

Administer four self-report instruments: Attachment Style Questionnaire (13 items of avoidance & anxiety), Trait Anxiety Inventory (20 items), 24-item Openness to Experience scale, and 7-item Leadership-Shaping Experiences scale.
Reverse-score and sum items to create scale scores; parcel items randomly into three indicators per latent construct.
Test measurement model for convergent/discriminant validity (CFI=.996, RMSEA=.02).
Estimate structural model (Model 1) with secure attachment predicting trait anxiety and openness; these predicting leadership experiences.
Compare fit with five alternative causal models using χ² difference tests and fit indices.
method.models: Structural Equation Modeling (SEM) with maximum likelihood estimation in AMOS 5
method.independent_variables: Secure attachment style, Trait anxiety, Openness to experience
method.tools_software: AMOS 5; (data preparation likely in SPSS, not explicitly stated)

results.summary: The best-fitting model showed that secure attachment style is negatively related to trait anxiety (β = −0.62, p < .01) and positively related to openness to experience (β = 0.24, p < .01). In turn, lower trait anxiety (β = −0.23, p < .01) and higher openness to experience (β = 0.34, p < .01) predicted greater leadership experiences. The model accounted for 19% of the variance in leadership experiences. Alternative models adding direct paths or reversing causal order fit less well or produced non-significant pathways.

metadata.original_paper_title: Attachment and leader's development via experiences
